title: Confidence intervals
intro: A single sample mean is a point estimate, and it is almost surely slightly off. It is more honest to state not a point but a range in which the true value plausibly lies. That is a confidence interval. But what exactly does "95%" mean?

beats.text: The white dashed line is the true value (unknown to us in real life). Take a sample: it yields a mean and an interval around it. Press "take a sample" a few times — each segment is the interval from one sample.

The intervals differ, because samples are random. Color shows the outcome: a blue interval covered the true value, a red one missed. The legend under the chart keeps score: how many covered, how many did not.
beats.predict: If you collect many samples, what share of their intervals will cover the true value?
beats.reveal: About 95% (for a 95% interval). Press "take 20" a few times: red (missing) ones are roughly 1 in 20, the rest blue. The counter confirms ≈ 95%. Switch the level to 90% or 99% — both the coverage share and the interval width change.

beats.text: Here is the precise meaning of "95% confidence": not "the true value is inside this interval with probability 95%", but "if the experiment were repeated many times, 95% of the constructed intervals would cover the truth". A specific interval either covered it or did not — we just do not know which.

Where does the interval's width come from? Under the chart is the formula: CI = x̄ ± z·SE, where SE = σ/√n and z sets the confidence level (95% → z = 1.96 — that same ≈2σ from the three-sigma rule). So the interval is "point ± margin", where the margin shrinks with less data spread and more sample. For a share (A/B conversion) the same idea: p̂ ± z·√(p̂(1−p̂)/n) — that is the "±2%" in A/B reports.

The larger the sample, the narrower the intervals (recall the bell of means tightening). A narrow interval — a precise estimate; a wide one — big uncertainty. A result without an interval is half the truth: "conversion 12%" and "12% ± 1%" versus "12% ± 9%" are completely different grounds for a decision, though the point is the same.

definitions.text: a single number — e.g. the sample mean. Almost always slightly off the truth.
a range around the estimate built so that, over repeated experiments, a set share of intervals (e.g. 95%) covers the true value.
that very share (95%, 99%): how often the method produces a covering interval over the long run.

practice: That is why reports say not "conversion 12%" but "12% ± 2%": the interval honestly shows how confident we are. Two estimates with overlapping intervals are too early to tell apart — we'll see this in the A/B test lesson.
And remember: a wide interval is not an error but a signal that "data is scarce". Want it narrower — collect more observations.
realLife: Confidence intervals stand behind the "margin of error" in poll coverage ("45% ± 3%"), behind forecast ranges and error bars on dashboard charts.
It is the same thing everywhere: don't pretend the estimate is exact — show its uncertainty honestly.
assumptions: The formula CI = x̄ ± z·SE relies on the normality of the sampling distribution (the CLT) and a sample-based estimate of σ; for small n use the t-distribution instead of z (slightly wider). For a share you need enough successes and failures.
The interval captures only RANDOM error. Systematic bias (an unrepresentative sample) is invisible to it — you can get a narrow but wrong interval around a biased estimate.
nextLabel: What if the data is weird and there is no formula? An interval by reshuffling → The bootstrap
